name: Floyd County
narrative: Elite Catastrophe is based in Floyd County, so this is the ground we know best. The county wraps around Rome where the Oostanaula, Etowah, and Coosa rivers meet, and the mix of hillside neighborhoods, river-bottom yards, and rural acreage gives it a heavy and varied tree load. Water oaks and red oaks shade the older in-town lots, many of them taller than the houses beneath them and prone to hollowing out with age. Out in the bottoms and along the ridges, loblolly and shortleaf pines stand in red clay that loses its grip after a soaking rain. Sweetgums scatter wood in every summer blow. Because we live here, Floyd County sees our crews more than anywhere else: removals in tight yards, structural pruning, deadwooding older hardwoods, and stump grinding. When severe weather crosses the county, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7. Most days, though, the work is calmer and planned well ahead.

name: Polk County
narrative: South and east of Rome, Polk County trades river bottoms for rolling farmland and the old mill towns of Cedartown and Rockmart. The canopy here is classic Northwest Georgia: red oaks and water oaks over the in-town streets, sweetgums along the fencerows, and thick stands of loblolly and shortleaf pine ringing the edges of both towns and filling the newer subdivisions. Those leftover pines are the county's most common hazard, tall and shallow-rooted in clay that softens after a long rain. Mature hardwoods carry their own risk, declining from the inside while looking full and green from the road. Elite Catastrophe works Polk County every week from our Rome base, handling removals, crown reduction, clearance pruning, and stump grinding. Anyone who has walked the Silver Comet Trail knows how much of the county's character rides on its trees. When storms tear through, our crews run 24/7 until the calls stop.

name: Gordon County
narrative: Gordon County sits along the I-75 corridor north of Rome, built around Calhoun and the smaller river towns like Resaca. The Oostanaula and its feeder creeks run wooded bottoms through the county, and those damp, low-lying stands are where clay stays soft the longest. On the higher ground, established neighborhoods hold mature water oaks and red oaks with limbs reaching over rooftops and drives, while the fast-growing corridor near the interstate keeps leaving tall loblolly pines exposed after the graders move on. Both patterns fail in the ways we see everywhere in Northwest Georgia: hardwoods hollowing out unseen, pines uprooting in saturated ground, sweetgums dropping wood in a stiff wind. Elite Catastrophe covers Gordon County on a regular schedule from Rome, bringing the same crew and equipment we use at home for removals, pruning, hazard checks, and stump grinding. When severe weather crosses the county, our storm crews respond 24/7.

name: Chattooga County
narrative: North of Rome the land climbs toward the Lookout Mountain foothills, and Chattooga County is where our trees get truly wind-tested. Summerville and Trion sit down in the valley, where storms funnel along the ridgelines and gusts pour over the shoulders of the slopes. Trees here anchor in thinner soil, lean harder toward the light, and lose crowns in a single rough afternoon. The species are the familiar ones: loblolly and shortleaf pine that snap or uproot in wet clay, water oaks that hollow with age, red oaks carrying heavy horizontal limbs, and sweetgums that shed wood in every blow. What the foothills add is exposure, which turns an ordinary leaner into a real hazard. Elite Catastrophe makes the drive up from Rome often, because Chattooga County homeowners live with some of the most storm-tested trees in our whole range. When the weather turns dangerous, our crews field storm calls 24/7.

name: Bartow County
narrative: East of Rome along I-75, Bartow County runs from the historic streets of Adairsville and Cartersville down to Lake Allatoona and the growing towns of Emerson and Euharlee. It is a county of contrasts for tree work. Old town centers keep generations-old oaks and sweetgums over close house lots, while newer subdivisions near the lake and the interstate leave tall leftover pines standing in graded, compacted clay. Mature water oaks here decline from the inside and drop heavy limbs on calm days. Construction-stressed pines look fine for a few summers, then brown out and turn brittle. Sweetgums split at weak unions and scatter wood every time the wind picks up. Elite Catastrophe covers Bartow County from our Rome base with the same family-owned crew we send through our own neighborhoods, handling removals, trimming, crown work, hazard evaluations, and stump grinding. When a storm system rakes across the county, our crews answer emergency calls 24/7.

name: Cherokee County
narrative: When a North Georgia storm line rakes across Cherokee County and leaves trees on roofs, across driveways, and down on power lines, Elite Catastrophe is the crew that answers. We are a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Cherokee County and its towns from Canton and Woodstock to Ball Ground and Nelson. Storm response is where we lead. The county carries a heavy tree load, from mature oaks over older neighborhoods to tall leftover pines in the newer subdivisions, all rooted in red clay that softens after a long rain, and when straight-line winds and hail get serious those trees fail into houses. Our emergency crews work structures first: the tree through the bedroom, the limb pinning a car, the trunk blocking the only way out. That line is answered around the clock. The rest of what we do is here year round too: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, storm damage cleanup, and roof tarping when a falling tree opens up the roof.

name: Cobb County
narrative: Cobb County sits southeast of Rome, and Elite Catastrophe serves it in full. When a spring or summer storm line rolls across the county and drops trees on roofs, across driveways, and onto power lines, our family-owned crew answers the call and gets on the road to the damage, available around the clock and year round. Storm work is where the urgency lives, so that is what we lead with: structures first, the tree through the roof, the limb pinning a car, the trunk blocking the only way out, plus storm damage cleanup and roof tarping to keep the weather out until repairs can follow. Cobb carries a heavy tree load, from the mature oaks over older Marietta and Smyrna neighborhoods to the leftover pines in newer south-county subdivisions, so the targets are everywhere. The everyday work is here too: tree removal, tree trimming, and stump grinding, whenever Cobb homeowners need it, storm or no storm.

name: Paulding County
narrative: Paulding County has filled in fast on the western side of metro Atlanta, trading open ground for wooded subdivisions where pines and hardwoods now stand close to houses. When a spring or summer storm saturates the red clay, pushes straight-line winds through, and drops trees on homes, driveways, and power lines, Elite Catastrophe answers. We are a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Dallas, Hiram, and the rest of Paulding County. Storm response leads the way here, and our emergency crews work structures first every time: trees on houses, limbs over doorways, trunks across the only road out. Those calls are answered around the clock. The county's red clay is the quiet culprit, holding water long after the rain stops so a loblolly pine has little to grip when the wind leans on it. Beyond the emergency, the full service list stays available year round: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, storm damage cleanup, and roof tarping when the roof takes a hit.
